# Numbers Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 170-171 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_018_page_001.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0126

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE DUTIES AND PORTIONS OF PRIESTS AND LEVITES:** Numbers 18 contains thirty-two verses and constitutes YHWH's definitive charter for the Aaronic priesthood and the Levitical service, spoken directly to Aaron — a rare and remarkable feature, since the vast majority of divine speech in the Torah is addressed to Moses. The chapter divides into four sections: the responsibility of priests and Levites for sanctuary trespass (vv. 1-7); the priestly portions from the holy offerings (vv. 8-20); the Levitical tithe as their inheritance (vv. 21-24); and the tithe of the tithe that the Levites must give to the priests (vv. 25-32). The Leningrad Codex presents this chapter across pages 170-171 (folios 85v-86r), continuing directly from the budding-rod narrative of chapter 17. The Vaticanus manuscript (pa_0126) preserves the LXX text of Numbers 18. Theologically, this chapter answers the crisis that has been building since chapter 16: Who may approach YHWH? What is the role of priests versus Levites? The answer is framed not as privilege but as burden — Aaron and his sons "shall bear the iniquity" (v. 1) of the sanctuary. The chapter's repeated phrase "a gift of service" (avodat mattanah, v. 6-7) defines the Levitical calling: their service is both a gift they give and a gift given to them. The chapter concludes with the striking statement that the Levites shall have no territorial inheritance in the land — YHWH Himself is their portion (v. 20). The setumah and petuhah spacing in the Leningrad Codex marks clear paragraph divisions at vv. 1, 8, 21, and 25, corresponding to the four major content sections.

---

## The Responsibility of Priests and Levites (vv. 1-7)

**1** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Aaron [Aharon], "You [attah] and-your-sons [u-vanekha] and-the-house of [u-veit] your-father [avikha] with-you [ittakh] shall-bear [tis'u] the-iniquity of [et avon] the-sanctuary [ha-miqdash], and-you [ve-attah] and-your-sons [u-vanekha] with-you [ittakh] shall-bear [tis'u] the-iniquity of [et avon] your-priesthood [kehunnat-khem].

> **Key terms:** וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן (va-yomer YHWH el Aharon) = "and YHWH said to Aaron" — a remarkable opening: YHWH speaks directly to Aaron, not through Moses. This is exceedingly rare in the Torah; most divine addresses are to Moses alone or to Moses and Aaron together. Here Aaron is the sole addressee, underscoring that this chapter concerns his priestly office specifically. אַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ וּבֵית־אָבִיךָ אִתָּךְ (attah u-vanekha u-veit avikha ittakh) = "you and your sons and the house of your father with you" — three concentric circles: Aaron himself, his sons (the priests), and "the house of your father" (the broader Levitical clan of Kohath). תִּשְׂאוּ אֶת־עֲוֹן הַמִּקְדָּשׁ (tis'u et avon ha-miqdash) = "you shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary" — the qal imperfect 2mp of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, bear, carry"). עֲוֹן (avon, "iniquity, guilt, punishment") from עוה (avah, "to bend, twist, distort"). The priests bear the liability for any breach of sanctuary holiness — if an unauthorized person trespasses, the guilt falls on the priests, not the trespasser. This is a burden, not a privilege. וְאַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ אִתָּךְ תִּשְׂאוּ אֶת־עֲוֹן כְּהֻנַּתְכֶם (ve-attah u-vanekha ittakh tis'u et avon kehunnat-khem) = "and you and your sons with you shall bear the iniquity of your priesthood" — the second clause narrows: Aaron and his sons alone (without the broader Levitical family) bear the specific guilt of any priestly error. כְּהֻנָּה (kehunnah, "priesthood") from כהן (kohen, "priest"). **LXX (18:1):** καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Ααρων λέγων Σὺ καὶ οἱ υἱοί σου καὶ ὁ οἶκος πατριᾶς σου μετὰ σοῦ λήμψεσθε τὰς ἁμαρτίας τῶν ἁγίων ("and the Lord said to Aaron, saying, You and your sons and the house of your father's family with you shall receive the sins of the holy things") — λαμβάνω ("receive/take") for tis'u; ἁμαρτίας τῶν ἁγίων ("sins of the holy things") for avon ha-miqdash.

**2** And-also [ve-gam] your-brothers [et akhekha], the-tribe of [matteh] Levi [Levi], the-tribe of [shevet] your-father [avikha], bring-near [haqrev] with-you [ittakh], and-they-shall-be-joined [ve-yillavu] to-you [alekha], and-they-shall-minister-to-you [vi-sharetukha]; and-you [ve-attah] and-your-sons [u-vanekha] with-you [ittakh] before [lifnei] the-Tent of [ohel] the-Testimony [ha-edut].

> **Key terms:** וְגַם אֶת־אַחֶיךָ מַטֵּה לֵוִי שֵׁבֶט אָבִיךָ (ve-gam et akhekha matteh Levi shevet avikha) = "and also your brothers, the tribe of Levi, the tribe of your father" — the Levites are called Aaron's "brothers" (אַחֶיךָ, akhekha), emphasizing kinship. Both מַטֶּה (matteh, "staff/tribe") and שֵׁבֶט (shevet, "rod/staff/tribe") are used synonymously for the tribal designation. הַקְרֵב אִתָּךְ (haqrev ittakh) = "bring near with you" — the hifil imperative of קרב (qarav, "to draw near"). The Levites are to be "brought near" — the very verb that was forbidden to unauthorized persons in chapter 16. Now it is commanded. וְיִלָּווּ עָלֶיךָ (ve-yillavu alekha) = "and they shall be joined to you" — the niphal of לוה (lavah, "to join, accompany"), a deliberate etymological wordplay on the name לֵוִי (Levi), echoing Leah's words at his birth: "now this time my husband will be joined to me" (Gen 29:34). The Levites fulfill their name: they are "the joined ones." וִישָׁרְתוּךָ (vi-sharetukha) = "and they shall minister to you" — the piel of שׁרת (sharat, "to minister, serve"), the verb of sacred service as distinct from עבד (avad, "to work/serve" in a general sense). לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל הָעֵדוּת (lifnei ohel ha-edut) = "before the Tent of the Testimony" — note הָעֵדוּת (ha-edut, "the testimony") rather than the more common מוֹעֵד (mo'ed, "meeting"). The Ark of the Testimony is housed within. **LXX:** καὶ τοὺς ἀδελφούς σου, φυλὴν Λευι, δῆμον τοῦ πατρός σου, προσαγάγου πρὸς σεαυτόν, καὶ προστεθήτωσάν σοι καὶ λειτουργήσουσίν σοι ("and your brothers, the tribe of Levi, the clan of your father, bring near to yourself, and let them be added to you and minister to you") — λειτουργέω ("perform public/sacred service") for vi-sharetukha.

**3** And-they-shall-keep [ve-shameru] your-charge [mishmart-ekha] and-the-charge of [u-mishmeret] all [kol] the-Tent [ha-ohel]; only [akh] unto [el] the-vessels of [kelei] the-Holy [ha-qodesh] and-unto [ve-el] the-altar [ha-mizbeakh] they-shall-not [lo] come-near [yiqravu], and-not [ve-lo] shall-they-die [yamutu], both-they [gam hem] and-also-you [gam attem].

> **Key terms:** וְשָׁמְרוּ מִשְׁמַרְתְּךָ וּמִשְׁמֶרֶת כָּל־הָאֹהֶל (ve-shameru mishmartekha u-mishmeret kol ha-ohel) = "and they shall keep your charge and the charge of all the Tent" — מִשְׁמֶרֶת (mishmeret, "charge, guard duty, watch") from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard, watch"). The Levites guard the Tent and perform duties on behalf of the priests. אַךְ אֶל־כְּלֵי הַקֹּדֶשׁ וְאֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לֹא יִקְרָבוּ (akh el kelei ha-qodesh ve-el ha-mizbeakh lo yiqravu) = "only, to the vessels of the Holy and to the altar they shall not come near" — אַךְ (akh, "only, surely, however") introduces the restrictive exception. The Levites may handle the outer work of the Tent but must not touch the sacred vessels or approach the altar — that is exclusively priestly. וְלֹא־יָמוּתוּ גַם־הֵם גַּם־אַתֶּם (ve-lo yamutu gam hem gam attem) = "and they shall not die — both they and also you" — the double גַּם...גַּם (gam...gam, "both...and also") creates a mutual warning: if the Levites trespass, both they and the priests who allowed it die. The boundary protects both parties. **LXX:** πλὴν πρὸς τὰ σκεύη τὰ ἅγια καὶ πρὸς τὸ θυσιαστήριον οὐ προσελεύσονται, καὶ οὐκ ἀποθανοῦνται καὶ οὗτοι καὶ ὑμεῖς ("except they shall not approach the holy vessels and the altar, and they shall not die — both these and you").

**4** And-they-shall-be-joined [ve-nilvu] to-you [alekha], and-they-shall-keep [ve-shameru] the-charge of [et mishmeret] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], for-all [le-khol] the-service of [avodat] the-Tent [ha-ohel]; and-a-stranger [ve-zar] shall-not [lo] come-near [yiqrav] to-you [alekhem].

> **Key terms:** וְנִלְווּ עָלֶיךָ (ve-nilvu alekha) = "and they shall be joined to you" — the niphal perfect consecutive of לוה (lavah, "to join"), repeating the wordplay on Levi from v. 2. וְשָׁמְרוּ אֶת־מִשְׁמֶרֶת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ve-shameru et mishmeret ohel mo'ed) = "and they shall keep the charge of the Tent of Meeting" — the Levites' primary role is guardianship. לְכֹל עֲבֹדַת הָאֹהֶל (le-khol avodat ha-ohel) = "for all the service of the Tent" — עֲבֹדָה (avodah, "service, labor, work") from עבד (avad, "to serve, work"). וְזָר לֹא־יִקְרַב אֲלֵיכֶם (ve-zar lo yiqrav alekhem) = "and a stranger shall not come near to you" — זָר (zar, "stranger, outsider") — any non-Levite. The Levites form a protective perimeter around the sacred precincts, ensuring that no unauthorized person approaches. This directly answers the people's terror in 17:27-28: "everyone who comes near to the tabernacle of YHWH dies!" The answer: the Levites will stand as a buffer. **LXX:** καὶ ἀλλογενὴς οὐ προσελεύσεται πρὸς σέ ("and a foreigner shall not come near to you") — ἀλλογενής ("of another race") for zar.

**5** And-you-shall-keep [u-shemartem] the-charge of [et mishmeret] the-Holy [ha-qodesh] and-the-charge of [ve-et mishmeret] the-altar [ha-mizbeakh], and-not [ve-lo] shall-there-be [yihyeh] again [od] wrath [qetsef] upon [al] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֵת מִשְׁמֶרֶת הַקֹּדֶשׁ וְאֵת מִשְׁמֶרֶת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (u-shemartem et mishmeret ha-qodesh ve-et mishmeret ha-mizbeakh) = "and you shall keep the charge of the Holy and the charge of the altar" — addressed to the priests specifically (2mp), distinguishing their charge (the inner sanctum and altar) from the Levites' charge (the outer Tent). וְלֹא־יִהְיֶה עוֹד קֶצֶף עַל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (ve-lo yihyeh od qetsef al benei Yisra'el) = "and there shall not be again wrath upon the Sons of Israel" — the purpose clause reveals the rationale for the entire priestly-Levitical system: the proper guarding of the sanctuary boundary prevents divine wrath from breaking out against the people. The קֶצֶף (qetsef, "wrath") of 17:11 that killed fourteen thousand seven hundred is still in view. The priestly and Levitical roles exist to prevent a repetition. **LXX:** καὶ οὐκ ἔσται ἁμαρτία ἐν υἱοῖς Ισραηλ ("and there shall not be sin among the sons of Israel") — ἁμαρτία ("sin") for qetsef ("wrath"), a notable interpretive shift: the LXX understands the priestly guard as preventing sin, not merely preventing wrath.

**6** And-I [va-ani], behold [hinneh], I-have-taken [laqakhti] your-brothers [et akheikhem] the-Levites [ha-Levi'im] from-the-midst of [mi-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; to-you [lakhem] a-gift [mattanah] they-are [hem], given [netunim] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], to-serve [la-avod] the-service of [et avodat] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** וַאֲנִי הִנֵּה לָקַחְתִּי אֶת־אֲחֵיכֶם הַלְוִיִּם מִתּוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-ani hinneh laqakhti et akheikhem ha-Levi'im mi-tokh benei Yisra'el) = "and I, behold, I have taken your brothers the Levites from the midst of the Sons of Israel" — YHWH Himself "took" the Levites — the qal perfect 1cs of לקח (laqakh, "to take"). The emphatic וַאֲנִי (va-ani, "and I") + הִנֵּה (hinneh, "behold") draws attention to the divine initiative. The Levites were not chosen by Aaron or by human arrangement; they were taken by YHWH. לָכֶם מַתָּנָה הֵם נְתוּנִם לַיהוָה (lakhem mattanah hem netunim la-YHWH) = "to you a gift they are, given to YHWH" — מַתָּנָה (mattanah, "gift") from נתן (natan, "to give"). נְתוּנִם (netunim, "given ones") — the qal passive participle of נתן, used substantively. The Levites are "given ones" — given to YHWH, and then given by YHWH to the priests. This is the origin of the later term נְתִינִים (netinim, "the given ones" / temple servants, Ezra 2:43). לַעֲבֹד אֶת־עֲבֹדַת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (la-avod et avodat ohel mo'ed) = "to serve the service of the Tent of Meeting" — the cognate accusative construction (עבד + עבודה) emphasizes the thoroughness of their service. **LXX:** κἀγὼ εἴληφα τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς ὑμῶν τοὺς Λευίτας ἐκ μέσου τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ δόμα δεδομένους κυρίῳ ("and I have taken your brothers the Levites from the midst of the sons of Israel, a gift given to the Lord") — δόμα δεδομένους ("a gift, given ones") for mattanah netunim.

**7** And-you [ve-attah] and-your-sons [u-vanekha] with-you [ittakh] shall-keep [tishmeru] your-priesthood [et kehunnat-khem] for-every [le-khol] matter of [devar] the-altar [ha-mizbeakh] and-for-within [u-le-mi-beit] the-veil [la-parokhet], and-you-shall-serve [va-avadtem]; a-service of [avodat] gift [mattanah] I-give [etten] your-priesthood [et kehunnat-khem], and-the-stranger [ve-ha-zar] who-comes-near [ha-qarev] shall-be-put-to-death [yumat].

> **Key terms:** וְאַתָּה וּבָנֶיךָ אִתְּךָ תִּשְׁמְרוּ אֶת־כְּהֻנַּתְכֶם (ve-attah u-vanekha ittekha tishmeru et kehunnat-khem) = "and you and your sons with you shall keep your priesthood" — the priestly duty is framed as "keeping" (שׁמר, shamar) — guarding, watching, maintaining. לְכָל־דְּבַר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וּלְמִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת (le-khol devar ha-mizbeakh u-le-mi-beit la-parokhet) = "for every matter of the altar and for within the veil" — the priestly sphere has two domains: the altar (in the outer court) and the space behind the פָּרֹכֶת (parokhet, "veil/curtain") — the inner sanctum. מִבֵּית (mi-beit, "from the house of" = "within, inside") + לַפָּרֹכֶת (la-parokhet, "the veil"). וַעֲבַדְתֶּם (va-avadtem) = "and you shall serve" — the qal perfect consecutive 2mp of עבד. עֲבֹדַת מַתָּנָה אֶתֵּן אֶת־כְּהֻנַּתְכֶם (avodat mattanah etten et kehunnat-khem) = "a service of gift I give your priesthood" — the priesthood is simultaneously a service (עֲבֹדָה, avodah) and a gift (מַתָּנָה, mattanah). YHWH "gives" (אֶתֵּן, etten, qal imperfect 1cs of נתן) the priesthood — it is not earned, inherited by right, or seized; it is a divine grant. וְהַזָּר הַקָּרֵב יוּמָת (ve-ha-zar ha-qarev yumat) = "and the stranger who comes near shall be put to death" — the hophal imperfect of מות (mut, "to die") — "shall surely be caused to die." The death penalty for trespass into the priestly sphere. This verse is the climactic statement of the entire priestly charter: the priesthood is a guarded gift, and its boundaries are lethal. **LXX:** λειτουργίαν δόματος τὴν ἱερατείαν ὑμῶν δίδωμι ὑμῖν, καὶ ὁ ἀλλογενὴς ὁ προσπορευόμενος ἀποθανεῖται ("a ministry of gift — your priesthood — I give to you, and the foreigner who approaches shall die").

---

## The Priestly Portions from the Holy Offerings (vv. 8-20)

**8** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Aaron [Aharon], "And-behold [ve-hinneh], I [ani] have-given [natatti] to-you [lekha] the-charge of [et mishmeret] my-contributions [terumotai] — for-all [le-khol] the-holy-things of [qodshei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], to-you [lekha] I-have-given-them [netattim] for-an-anointing [le-moshekhah] and-to-your-sons [u-le-vanekha] for-a-statute of [le-khoq] perpetuity [olam].

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן (va-yedabber YHWH el Aharon) = "and YHWH spoke to Aaron" — again directly to Aaron, continuing the unique address form of this chapter. וְהִנֵּה נָתַתִּי לְךָ אֶת־מִשְׁמֶרֶת תְּרוּמֹתָי (ve-hinneh natatti lekha et mishmeret terumotai) = "and behold, I have given to you the charge of my contributions" — תְּרוּמָה (terumah, "contribution, heave-offering") from רום (rum, "to be high, lift up") — that which is "lifted up" or set apart from the common. The suffix -ַי (ai, "my") makes these "my contributions" — YHWH's own property that He now assigns to the priests. לְכָל־קָדְשֵׁי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל (le-khol qodshei benei Yisra'el) = "for all the holy things of the Sons of Israel." לְךָ נְתַתִּים לְמָשְׁחָה (lekha netattim le-moshkhah) = "to you I have given them for an anointing" — לְמָשְׁחָה (le-moshkhah) is debated: it may mean "as an anointed portion" (i.e., the priestly share by right of their anointing), or "for greatness" (from a secondary sense of משׁח). Most literally: the portions belong to the priests because they are the anointed ones. וּלְבָנֶיךָ לְחָק־עוֹלָם (u-le-vanekha le-khoq olam) = "and to your sons for a statute of perpetuity" — חֹק (khoq, "statute, ordinance") + עוֹלָם (olam, "perpetuity, long duration, eternity"). An irrevocable divine decree. **LXX:** κἀγὼ ἰδοὺ δέδωκα ὑμῖν τὴν διατήρησιν τῶν ἀπαρχῶν ("and I, behold, I have given you the keeping of the firstfruits") — ἀπαρχαί ("firstfruits") for terumot; εἰς χρῖσμα ("for an anointing") for le-moshkhah.

**9** This [zeh] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lekha] from-the-holiest of [mi-qodesh] the-holy-things [ha-qodashim], from-the-fire [min ha-esh]: every [kol] offering-of-theirs [qorbanam], for-every [le-khol] grain-offering-of-theirs [minkhatam] and-for-every [u-le-khol] sin-offering-of-theirs [khattatam] and-for-every [u-le-khol] guilt-offering-of-theirs [ashamam] which [asher] they-shall-return [yashivu] to-me [li] — a-holiest of [qodesh] holy-things [qodashim] for-you [lekha] and-for-your-sons [u-le-vanekha].

> **Key terms:** זֶה־יִּהְיֶה לְךָ מִקֹּדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים מִן־הָאֵשׁ (zeh yihyeh lekha mi-qodesh ha-qodashim min ha-esh) = "this shall be for you from the holiest of the holy things, from the fire" — קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים (qodesh ha-qodashim, "holy of holies / most holy") designates the highest grade of sanctity. מִן־הָאֵשׁ (min ha-esh, "from the fire") indicates the portions that are not consumed on the altar but reserved from the fire-offerings. כָּל־קָרְבָּנָם לְכָל־מִנְחָתָם (kol qorbanam le-khol minkhatam) = "every offering of theirs, for every grain-offering of theirs" — קָרְבָּן (qorban, "offering, that which is brought near") from קרב; מִנְחָה (minkhah, "grain-offering, gift, tribute"). וּלְכָל־חַטָּאתָם וּלְכָל־אֲשָׁמָם (u-le-khol khattatam u-le-khol ashamam) = "and for every sin-offering of theirs and for every guilt-offering of theirs" — חַטָּאת (khattat, "sin-offering") from חטא; אָשָׁם (asham, "guilt-offering, trespass-offering") from אשׁם (asham, "to be guilty"). אֲשֶׁר יָשִׁיבוּ לִי (asher yashivu li) = "which they shall return to me" — the hifil of שׁוב (shuv, "to return"), a striking verb choice: the offerings are "returned" to YHWH, implying that everything belongs to Him in the first place. **LXX:** τοῦτο ἔσται ὑμῖν ἀπὸ τῶν ἡγιασμένων ἁγίων τῶν καρπωμάτων ("this shall be for you from the sanctified holy things, from the burnt offerings").

**10** In-the-holiest of [be-qodesh] the-holy [ha-qodashim] you-shall-eat-it [tokhlenu]; every [kol] male [zakhar] shall-eat [yokhal] it [oto] — holy [qodesh] it-shall-be [yihyeh] to-you [lakh].

> **Key terms:** בְּקֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים תֹּאכְלֶנּוּ (be-qodesh ha-qodashim tokhlenu) = "in the holiest of the holy you shall eat it" — the most holy offerings must be eaten within the sacred precincts (the court of the Tabernacle). כָּל־זָכָר יֹאכַל אֹתוֹ (kol zakhar yokhal oto) = "every male shall eat it" — only male priests may consume the most holy offerings. זָכָר (zakhar, "male") — the restriction to males applies to the most holy grade; lesser holy things may be shared with priestly families including women and children (cf. v. 11). קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה־לָּךְ (qodesh yihyeh lakh) = "holy it shall be to you" — the feminine לָּךְ (lakh, "to you") is addressed to Aaron directly (the 2ms form with the pausal lengthening). **LXX:** ἐν τῷ ἁγίῳ τῶν ἁγίων φάγεσθε αὐτά· πᾶν ἀρσενικὸν φάγεται αὐτά, ἅγια ἔσται σοι ("in the holy of holies you shall eat them; every male shall eat them, they shall be holy to you").

**11** And-this [ve-zeh] for-you [lekha]: the-contribution of [terumat] their-gift [mattanam] — for-all [le-khol] the-wave-offerings of [tenufot] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el], to-you [lekha] I-have-given-them [netattim], and-to-your-sons [u-le-vanekha] and-to-your-daughters [u-li-venotekha] with-you [ittakh] for-a-statute of [le-khoq] perpetuity [olam]; every [kol] clean-one [tahor] in-your-house [be-veitekha] shall-eat [yokhal] it [oto].

> **Key terms:** וְזֶה־לְּךָ תְּרוּמַת מַתָּנָם (ve-zeh lekha terumat mattanam) = "and this for you: the contribution of their gift" — a second category of priestly income now begins: the wave-offerings (תְּנוּפֹת, tenufot), which are of a lesser holiness than the offerings in vv. 9-10. לְכָל־תְּנוּפֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (le-khol tenufot benei Yisra'el) = "for all the wave-offerings of the Sons of Israel" — תְּנוּפָה (tenufah, "wave-offering") from נוף (nuf, "to wave, swing"), the ritual waving of portions before YHWH. לְךָ נְתַתִּים וּלְבָנֶיךָ וְלִבְנֹתֶיךָ אִתְּךָ (lekha netattim u-le-vanekha u-li-venotekha ittakh) = "to you I have given them, and to your sons and to your daughters with you" — critically, daughters are now included. The wave-offerings, being of lesser holiness, may be eaten by the entire priestly household, not just males. לְחָק עוֹלָם (le-khoq olam) = "for a statute of perpetuity." כָּל־טָהוֹר בְּבֵיתְךָ יֹאכַל אֹתוֹ (kol tahor be-veitekha yokhal oto) = "every clean one in your house shall eat it" — טָהוֹר (tahor, "clean, pure") from טהר (taher, "to be clean/pure"). The condition is ritual purity, not gender. **LXX:** πᾶς καθαρὸς ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ σου φάγεται αὐτά ("everyone clean in your house shall eat them").

**12** All [kol] the-best of [khelev] the-oil [yitshar] and-all [ve-khol] the-best of [khelev] the-new-wine [tirosh] and-the-grain [ve-dagan], their-firstfruits [reshitam] which [asher] they-shall-give [yittenu] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], to-you [lekha] I-have-given-them [netattim].

> **Key terms:** כֹּל חֵלֶב יִצְהָר וְכָל־חֵלֶב תִּירוֹשׁ וְדָגָן (kol khelev yitshar ve-khol khelev tirosh ve-dagan) = "all the best of the oil and all the best of the new wine and the grain" — חֵלֶב (khelev, "fat, best part") — though commonly "fat," here it means "the richest, finest, choicest portion." יִצְהָר (yitshar, "fresh oil") — specifically olive oil in its purest form. תִּירוֹשׁ (tirosh, "new wine, must") — freshly pressed grape juice, not yet fermented. דָּגָן (dagan, "grain, cereal") — the staple crop. Together, oil, wine, and grain represent the three pillars of agricultural wealth in ancient Israel. רֵאשִׁיתָם אֲשֶׁר־יִתְּנוּ לַיהוָה (reshitam asher yittenu la-YHWH) = "their firstfruits which they shall give to YHWH" — רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "first, beginning, firstfruits") from ראשׁ (rosh, "head"). The first and best of the harvest belongs to YHWH and is given to the priests. **LXX:** πᾶσα ἀπαρχὴ ἐλαίου καὶ πᾶσα ἀπαρχὴ οἴνου καὶ σίτου, ἀπαρχὴ αὐτῶν ὅσα ἂν δῶσιν τῷ κυρίῳ, σοὶ δέδωκα αὐτά ("every firstfruit of oil and every firstfruit of wine and grain — their firstfruit, whatever they give to the Lord — to you I have given them").

**13** The-firstfruits of [bikkurei] all [kol] which [asher] is-in-their-land [be-artsam] which [asher] they-shall-bring [yavi'u] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakh]; every [kol] clean-one [tahor] in-your-house [be-veitekha] shall-eat [yokhal] it.

> **Key terms:** בִּכּוּרֵי כָּל־אֲשֶׁר בְּאַרְצָם (bikkurei kol asher be-artsam) = "the firstfruits of all that is in their land" — בִּכּוּרִים (bikkurim, "firstfruits, first ripe produce") from בכר (bakhar, "to be firstborn, bear early fruit"), distinct from רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "firstfruits" in v. 12). The bikkurim are the first-ripening produce physically brought to the sanctuary (cf. Deut 26:1-11). אֲשֶׁר־יָבִיאוּ לַיהוָה (asher yavi'u la-YHWH) = "which they shall bring to YHWH" — the hifil of בוא (bo, "to come/bring"). לָךְ יִהְיֶה (lakh yihyeh) = "for you it shall be." כָּל־טָהוֹר בְּבֵיתְךָ יֹאכְלֶנּוּ (kol tahor be-veitekha yokhlennu) = "every clean one in your house shall eat it" — the same condition as v. 11. **LXX:** πᾶς καθαρὸς ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ σου φάγεται αὐτά ("everyone clean in your house shall eat them").

**14** Every [kol] devoted-thing [kherem] in-Israel [be-Yisra'el] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakh].

> **Key terms:** כָּל־חֵרֶם בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל לְךָ יִהְיֶה (kol kherem be-Yisra'el lekha yihyeh) = "every devoted thing in Israel shall be for you" — חֵרֶם (kherem, "devoted thing, ban") from חרם (kharam, "to devote, ban, consecrate to destruction"). The kherem is the most extreme form of consecration: something irrevocably devoted to YHWH, which can never be redeemed or returned to common use (Lev 27:28-29). Fields, animals, or property placed under kherem pass permanently to the priesthood. This is distinct from voluntary offerings — it is total, irrevocable dedication. **LXX:** πᾶν ἀνάθεμα ἐν υἱοῖς Ισραηλ σοὶ ἔσται ("every anathema among the sons of Israel shall be yours") — ἀνάθεμα ("that which is set up/devoted") for kherem.

**15** Every [kol] opener of [peter] a-womb [rekhem] of-all [le-khol] flesh [basar] which [asher] they-shall-bring-near [yaqrivu] to-YHWH [la-YHWH], among-man [ba-adam] and-among-beast [u-va-behemah], shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakh]; however [akh], you-shall-surely-redeem [padoh tifdeh] the-firstborn of [bekhor] man [ha-adam], and-the-firstborn of [ve-et bekhor] the-unclean [ha-temei'ah] beast [ha-behemah] you-shall-redeem [tifdeh].

> **Key terms:** כָּל־פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם לְכָל־בָּשָׂר (kol peter rekhem le-khol basar) = "every opener of a womb, of all flesh" — פֶּטֶר (peter, "opener, firstling") from פטר (patar, "to open, let go, release"). רֶחֶם (rekhem, "womb"). The firstborn of every creature belongs to YHWH (Exod 13:2, 12). בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה (ba-adam u-va-behemah) = "among man and among beast" — this applies universally to human and animal firstborn. אַךְ פָּדֹה תִפְדֶּה אֵת בְּכוֹר הָאָדָם (akh padoh tifdeh et bekhor ha-adam) = "however, you shall surely redeem the firstborn of man" — the infinitive absolute + imperfect construction (פָּדֹה תִפְדֶּה, padoh tifdeh) creates emphasis: "redeeming, you shall redeem" = "you shall surely/certainly redeem." פדה (padah, "to redeem, ransom") — human firstborn cannot be sacrificed; they must be redeemed with a monetary payment. וְאֵת בְּכוֹר־הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּמֵאָה תִּפְדֶּה (ve-et bekhor ha-behemah ha-temei'ah tifdeh) = "and the firstborn of the unclean beast you shall redeem" — the firstborn of unclean animals (donkeys, camels, etc.) must also be redeemed since they cannot be sacrificed. **LXX:** πλὴν λύτρα λυτρώσῃ τὰ πρωτότοκα τῶν ἀνθρώπων ("but with a ransom you shall ransom the firstborn of men") — λύτρα λυτρώσῃ rendering the infinitive absolute construction.

**16** And-their-redeemed-ones [u-feduyav]: from-a-son of [mi-ben] a-month [khodesh] you-shall-redeem-them [tifdeim] by-your-valuation [be-erkekha], silver [kesef] of-five [khamesh] shekels [sheqalim] by-the-shekel of [be-sheqel] the-holy [ha-qodesh] — twenty [esrim] gerahs [gerah] it [hu].

> **Key terms:** וּפְדוּיָו מִבֶּן־חֹדֶשׁ תִּפְדֶּם (u-feduyav mi-ben khodesh tifdeim) = "and his redeemed ones: from a son of a month you shall redeem them" — פְּדוּיִם (peduyim, "redeemed ones") — the qal passive participle of פדה. מִבֶּן־חֹדֶשׁ (mi-ben khodesh, "from a son of a month") = from one month old. The firstborn is redeemed at one month, not at birth. בְּעֶרְכְּךָ כֶּסֶף חֲמֵשֶׁת שְׁקָלִים (be-erkekha kesef khameshet sheqalim) = "by your valuation, silver of five shekels" — עֵרֶךְ (erekh, "valuation, assessment") from ערך (arakh, "to arrange, set in order, value"). The redemption price is five shekels of silver — the standard pidyon ha-ben ("redemption of the son") payment still practiced in Judaism today. בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ עֶשְׂרִים גֵּרָה הוּא (be-sheqel ha-qodesh esrim gerah hu) = "by the shekel of the holy — twenty gerahs it is" — the sanctuary shekel standard, with the parenthetical clarification that one shekel = twenty gerahs (a gerah being approximately 0.57 grams of silver). **LXX:** καὶ ἡ λύτρωσις αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ μηνιαίου, ἡ συντίμησις πέντε σίκλων ("and its redemption from a month old — the assessment: five shekels").

**17** However [akh], the-firstborn of [bekhor] an-ox [shor], or [o] the-firstborn of [bekhor] a-sheep [kesev], or [o] the-firstborn of [bekhor] a-goat [ez], you-shall-not [lo] redeem [tifdeh] — holy [qodesh] they [hem]; their-blood [et damam] you-shall-sprinkle [tizroq] upon [al] the-altar [ha-mizbeakh], and-their-fat [ve-et khelabam] you-shall-turn-to-smoke [taqtir] as-a-fire-offering [ishsheh] for-a-pleasing-aroma [le-re'akh nikhoakh] to-YHWH [la-YHWH].

> **Key terms:** אַךְ בְּכוֹר־שׁוֹר אוֹ־בְכוֹר כֶּשֶׂב אוֹ־בְכוֹר עֵז (akh bekhor shor o bekhor kesev o bekhor ez) = "however, the firstborn of an ox, or the firstborn of a sheep, or the firstborn of a goat" — the three categories of clean sacrificial animals. שׁוֹר (shor, "ox, bull"), כֶּשֶׂב (kesev, "young ram, lamb"), עֵז (ez, "goat"). לֹא תִפְדֶּה קֹדֶשׁ הֵם (lo tifdeh qodesh hem) = "you shall not redeem — holy they are" — unlike human and unclean-animal firstborn, clean-animal firstborn are sacrificed, not redeemed. אֶת־דָּמָם תִּזְרֹק עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (et damam tizroq al ha-mizbeakh) = "their blood you shall sprinkle upon the altar" — the qal imperfect of זרק (zaraq, "to sprinkle, toss, dash"). The blood manipulation is the central sacrificial act. וְאֶת־חֶלְבָּם תַּקְטִיר (ve-et khelbam taqtir) = "and their fat you shall turn to smoke" — the hifil of קטר (qatar, "to send up smoke"). אִשֶּׁה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה (ishsheh le-re'akh nikhoakh la-YHWH) = "a fire-offering for a pleasing aroma to YHWH" — אִשֶּׁה (ishsheh, "fire-offering") + רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'akh nikhoakh, "aroma of soothing/pleasing"), the anthropomorphic language of YHWH "smelling" the offering with satisfaction. **LXX:** τὸ αἷμα αὐτῶν προσχεεῖς πρὸς τὸ θυσιαστήριον, καὶ τὸ στέαρ ἀνοίσεις κάρπωμα εἰς ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας κυρίῳ ("their blood you shall pour out at the altar, and their fat you shall offer up as a burnt offering for a sweet-smelling aroma to the Lord").

**18** And-their-flesh [u-vesaram] shall-be [yihyeh] for-you [lakh] — like-the-breast of [ke-khazeh] the-wave-offering [ha-tenufah] and-like-the-thigh of [u-khe-shoq] the-right [ha-yamin] — for-you [lekha] it-shall-be [yihyeh].

> **Key terms:** וּבְשָׂרָם יִהְיֶה לָּךְ (u-vesaram yihyeh lakh) = "and their flesh shall be for you" — the meat of the firstborn clean animals (after blood and fat are offered) belongs to the priest. כַּחֲזֵה הַתְּנוּפָה וּכְשׁוֹק הַיָּמִין (ke-khazeh ha-tenufah u-khe-shoq ha-yamin) = "like the breast of the wave-offering and like the thigh of the right" — the comparison is to the peace-offering portions: the חָזֶה (khazeh, "breast") that is waved and the שׁוֹק (shoq, "thigh, leg") of the right side (cf. Lev 7:31-34). Just as those portions belong to the priests from peace-offerings, so the flesh of firstborn animals belongs to the priests. **LXX:** καὶ τὰ κρέα ἔσται σοί, κατὰ τὸ στηθύνιον τοῦ ἐπιθέματος καὶ κατὰ τὸν βραχίονα τὸν δεξιόν σοὶ ἔσται ("and the flesh shall be yours — like the breast of the wave-offering and like the right arm, it shall be yours").

**19** All [kol] the-contributions of [terumot] the-holy-things [ha-qodashim] which [asher] shall-lift-up [yarimu] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] I-have-given [natatti] to-you [lekha] and-to-your-sons [u-le-vanekha] and-to-your-daughters [u-li-venotekha] with-you [ittakh] for-a-statute of [le-khoq] perpetuity [olam] — a-covenant of [berit] salt [melakh] perpetual [olam] it-is [hi] before [lifnei] YHWH, for-you [lekha] and-for-your-seed [u-le-zar'akha] with-you [ittakh]."

> **Key terms:** כֹּל תְּרוּמֹת הַקֳּדָשִׁים (kol terumot ha-qodashim) = "all the contributions of the holy things" — a comprehensive summary of all the priestly portions enumerated in vv. 8-18. בְּרִית מֶלַח עוֹלָם הִוא לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (berit melakh olam hi lifnei YHWH) = "a covenant of salt, perpetual, it is before YHWH" — בְּרִית (berit, "covenant") + מֶלַח (melakh, "salt"). The "covenant of salt" is one of the Torah's most striking covenant metaphors. Salt preserves, prevents decay, and was universally used in ancient Near Eastern covenant meals and treaty ratification ceremonies. A "salt covenant" is one that cannot spoil or be broken — it is incorruptible and eternal (cf. 2 Chron 13:5, where the Davidic covenant is also called a "covenant of salt"). לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אִתָּךְ (lekha u-le-zar'akha ittakh) = "for you and for your seed with you" — the priestly inheritance extends to all future generations. **LXX:** διαθήκη ἁλὸς αἰωνίου ἐστὶν ἔναντι κυρίου σοὶ καὶ τῷ σπέρματί σου ("a covenant of eternal salt it is before the Lord, for you and for your seed") — διαθήκη ἁλός ("covenant of salt") translating berit melakh precisely.

**20** And-said [va-yomer] YHWH unto [el] Aaron [Aharon], "In-their-land [be-artsam] you-shall-not [lo] have-inheritance [tinkhal], and-a-portion [ve-kheleq] you-shall-not [lo] have [yihyeh] for-you [lekha] in-their-midst [be-tokham]; I [ani] am-your-portion [khelqekha] and-your-inheritance [ve-nakhalatekha] in-the-midst of [be-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן בְּאַרְצָם לֹא תִנְחָל (va-yomer YHWH el Aharon be-artsam lo tinkhal) = "and YHWH said to Aaron, In their land you shall not have inheritance" — the qal imperfect of נחל (nakhal, "to inherit, possess as inheritance"). The priests receive no tribal territory in the land of Canaan. וְחֵלֶק לֹא־יִהְיֶה לְךָ בְּתוֹכָם (ve-kheleq lo yihyeh lekha be-tokham) = "and a portion shall not be for you in their midst" — חֵלֶק (kheleq, "portion, share, allotment") from חלק (khalaq, "to divide, apportion"). The double negation (no inheritance, no portion) is emphatic. אֲנִי חֶלְקְךָ וְנַחֲלָתְךָ (ani khelqekha ve-nakhalatekha) = "I am your portion and your inheritance" — one of the most theologically profound statements in the entire Torah. YHWH Himself replaces land, territory, and material inheritance. The God who gives the land to the other tribes gives Himself to the priests. חֵלֶק (kheleq) and נַחֲלָה (nakhalah, "inheritance, possession") from נחל — both terms of territorial allotment are applied directly to YHWH. This becomes the foundation of all later theology of "YHWH as my portion" (Ps 16:5; 73:26; 119:57; 142:6; Lam 3:24). **LXX:** ἐγὼ μερίς σου καὶ κληρονομία σου ἐν μέσῳ τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ("I am your portion and your inheritance in the midst of the sons of Israel") — μερίς ("portion") + κληρονομία ("inheritance") for kheleq + nakhalah.

## The Levitical Tithe (vv. 21-24)

**21** And-to-the-sons of [ve-li-venei] Levi [Levi], behold [hinneh], I-have-given [natatti] every [kol] tithe [ma'aser] in-Israel [be-Yisra'el] for-an-inheritance [le-nakhalah], in-exchange-for [khelef] their-service [avodatam] which [asher] they [hem] are-serving [ovedim] — the-service of [et avodat] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** וְלִבְנֵי לֵוִי הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי כָּל־מַעֲשֵׂר בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל לְנַחֲלָה (ve-li-venei Levi hinneh natatti kol ma'aser be-Yisra'el le-nakhalah) = "and to the sons of Levi, behold, I have given every tithe in Israel for an inheritance" — מַעֲשֵׂר (ma'aser, "tithe, tenth") from עשׂר (eser, "ten"). The Levites receive a tenth of all Israelite produce as their livelihood, replacing the territorial inheritance they do not receive. חֵלֶף עֲבֹדָתָם אֲשֶׁר הֵם עֹבְדִים (khelef avodatam asher hem ovedim) = "in exchange for their service which they are serving" — חֵלֶף (khelef, "in exchange for, in return for, instead of") — a rare word indicating compensation or substitution. The tithe is not charity; it is wages for sacred labor. אֶת־עֲבֹדַת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (et avodat ohel mo'ed) = "the service of the Tent of Meeting" — the cognate accusative construction again. **LXX:** καὶ τοῖς υἱοῖς Λευι ἰδοὺ δέδωκα πᾶν ἐπιδέκατον ἐν Ισραηλ ἐν κλήρῳ ("and to the sons of Levi, behold, I have given every tenth in Israel as a lot/inheritance") — ἐπιδέκατον ("tenth") for ma'aser.

**22** And-not [ve-lo] shall-come-near [yiqrevu] again [od] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] unto [el] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], to-bear [laset] sin [khet] to-die [lamut].

> **Key terms:** וְלֹא־יִקְרְבוּ עוֹד בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ve-lo yiqrevu od benei Yisra'el el ohel mo'ed) = "and the Sons of Israel shall not come near again to the Tent of Meeting" — the prohibition established: ordinary Israelites may not approach the Tent. This answers the terrified cry of 17:27-28: "Behold, we perish! We are lost! We are all lost! Everyone who comes near — who comes near to the tabernacle of YHWH — dies! Shall we ever cease perishing?" The answer is: the Levites will serve as a buffer, and the people will not come near. לָשֵׂאת חֵטְא לָמוּת (laset khet lamut) = "to bear sin, to die" — the infinitive construct of נשׂא (nasa, "to bear") + חֵטְא (khet, "sin") + the infinitive of מות (mut, "to die"). If they approach, they bear lethal sin. **LXX:** καὶ οὐ προσελεύσονται ἔτι οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ εἰς τὴν σκηνὴν τοῦ μαρτυρίου λαβεῖν ἁμαρτίαν θανατηφόρον ("and the sons of Israel shall no longer approach the tent of the testimony to receive death-bearing sin") — θανατηφόρον ("death-bearing") combines the two Hebrew infinitives into a single adjective.

**23** And-shall-serve [ve-avad] the-Levite [ha-Levi] — he [hu] — the-service of [et avodat] the-Tent of [ohel] Meeting [mo'ed], and-they [ve-hem] shall-bear [yis'u] their-iniquity [avonam]; a-statute of [khuqqat] perpetuity [olam] throughout-your-generations [le-dorotekhem]; and-in-the-midst of [u-ve-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] they-shall-not [lo] inherit [yinkhal] an-inheritance [nakhalah].

> **Key terms:** וְעָבַד הַלֵּוִי הוּא אֶת־עֲבֹדַת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ve-avad ha-Levi hu et avodat ohel mo'ed) = "and the Levite — he — shall serve the service of the Tent of Meeting" — the emphatic הוּא (hu, "he") isolates the Levite as the sole performer of Tent service. וְהֵם יִשְׂאוּ עֲוֹנָם (ve-hem yis'u avonam) = "and they shall bear their iniquity" — the Levites bear the liability for any error in their service, parallel to the priests bearing the iniquity of the sanctuary (v. 1). חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (khuqqat olam le-dorotekhem) = "a statute of perpetuity throughout your generations" — חֻקָּה (khuqqah, "statute, ordinance") + עוֹלָם + לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (le-dorotekhem, "for your generations") from דּוֹר (dor, "generation"). וּבְתוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא יִנְחֲלוּ נַחֲלָה (u-ve-tokh benei Yisra'el lo yinkhalu nakhalah) = "and in the midst of the Sons of Israel they shall not inherit an inheritance" — the cognate accusative (נחל + נחלה) emphasizes the totality of the exclusion. Like the priests (v. 20), the Levites receive no territorial inheritance. **LXX:** καὶ ἐν μέσῳ υἱῶν Ισραηλ οὐ κληρονομήσουσιν κληρονομίαν ("and in the midst of the sons of Israel they shall not inherit an inheritance").

**24** For [ki] the-tithe of [et ma'sar] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] which [asher] they-shall-lift-up [yarimu] to-YHWH [la-YHWH] as-a-contribution [terumah] I-have-given [natatti] to-the-Levites [la-Levi'im] for-an-inheritance [le-nakhalah]; therefore [al ken] I-have-said [amarti] to-them [lahem], 'In-the-midst of [be-tokh] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] they-shall-not [lo] inherit [yinkhalu] an-inheritance [nakhalah].'"

> **Key terms:** כִּי אֶת־מַעְשַׂר בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר־יָרִימוּ לַיהוָה תְּרוּמָה (ki et ma'sar benei Yisra'el asher yarimu la-YHWH terumah) = "for the tithe of the Sons of Israel which they shall lift up to YHWH as a contribution" — the tithe is itself a תְּרוּמָה (terumah, "contribution, heave-offering"), something "raised up" and set apart. נָתַתִּי לַלְוִיִּם לְנַחֲלָה (natatti la-Levi'im le-nakhalah) = "I have given to the Levites for an inheritance" — the tithe replaces land. עַל־כֵּן אָמַרְתִּי לָהֶם (al ken amarti lahem) = "therefore I have said to them" — עַל כֵּן (al ken, "therefore, on account of this") introduces the logical conclusion. The tithe is the reason for the landlessness, and the landlessness is the reason for the tithe — a reciprocal arrangement. **LXX:** ὅτι τὰ ἐπιδέκατα τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ, ὅσα ἂν ἀφορίσωσιν κυρίῳ ἀφαίρεμα, δέδωκα τοῖς Λευίταις ἐν κλήρῳ ("for the tithes of the sons of Israel, whatever they set apart for the Lord as a contribution, I have given to the Levites as an inheritance").

## The Tithe of the Tithe (vv. 25-32)

**25** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh le'mor) = "and YHWH spoke to Moses, saying" — the addressee shifts back to Moses. The instructions about the Levitical tithe-of-the-tithe are given to Moses (who will convey them to the Levites), whereas the priestly charter (vv. 1-24) was given directly to Aaron. **LXX:** καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων ("and the Lord spoke to Moses, saying").

**26** "And-unto [ve-el] the-Levites [ha-Levi'im] you-shall-speak [tedabber], and-you-shall-say [ve-amarta] unto [aleihem] them, 'When [ki] you-take [tiqkhu] from-the-Sons of [me-et benei] Israel [Yisra'el] the-tithe [et ha-ma'aser] which [asher] I-have-given [natatti] to-you [lakhem] from-them [me-ittam] for-your-inheritance [be-nakhalat-khem], and-you-shall-lift-up [va-haremothem] from-it [mi-mennu] a-contribution of [terumat] YHWH — a-tithe [ma'aser] from [min] the-tithe [ha-ma'aser].

> **Key terms:** וְאֶל־הַלְוִיִּם תְּדַבֵּר (ve-el ha-Levi'im tedabber) = "and to the Levites you shall speak" — Moses is the intermediary for instructions to the Levites. כִּי־תִקְחוּ מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַמַּעֲשֵׂר (ki tiqkhu me-et benei Yisra'el et ha-ma'aser) = "when you take from the Sons of Israel the tithe" — the Levites receive the tithe from Israel (vv. 21-24), but they in turn owe a tithe of that tithe. וַהֲרֵמֹתֶם מִמֶּנּוּ תְּרוּמַת יְהוָה מַעֲשֵׂר מִן־הַמַּעֲשֵׂר (va-haremothem mi-mennu terumat YHWH ma'aser min ha-ma'aser) = "and you shall lift up from it a contribution of YHWH — a tithe from the tithe" — the hifil perfect consecutive 2mp of רום (rum, "to lift up, raise"). The expression מַעֲשֵׂר מִן־הַמַּעֲשֵׂר (ma'aser min ha-ma'aser, "a tithe from the tithe") = one tenth of the tenth = one hundredth of the original produce. This goes to the priests (v. 28). The Levites are not exempt from giving; they too contribute from what they receive. **LXX:** ἀφαίρεμα κυρίῳ, ἐπιδέκατον ἀπὸ τοῦ ἐπιδεκάτου ("a contribution to the Lord: a tenth from the tenth").

**27** And-it-shall-be-reckoned [ve-nekhshav] to-you [lakhem] your-contribution [terumat-khem] as-the-grain [ka-dagan] from [min] the-threshing-floor [ha-goren] and-as-the-fullness [ve-kha-mele'ah] from [min] the-winepress [ha-yaqev].

> **Key terms:** וְנֶחְשַׁב לָכֶם תְּרוּמַתְכֶם (ve-nekhshav lakhem terumat-khem) = "and it shall be reckoned to you, your contribution" — the niphal perfect consecutive of חשׁב (khashav, "to think, reckon, account"). The Levites' tithe-of-the-tithe will be "reckoned" or "credited" to them as though they were landowners giving from their own harvest. כַּדָּגָן מִן־הַגֹּרֶן (ka-dagan min ha-goren) = "as the grain from the threshing-floor" — דָּגָן (dagan, "grain") + גֹּרֶן (goren, "threshing-floor"), the place where grain is separated from chaff. וְכַמְּלֵאָה מִן־הַיָּקֶב (ve-kha-mele'ah min ha-yaqev) = "and as the fullness from the winepress" — מְלֵאָה (mele'ah, "fullness, what fills") from מלא (male, "to be full") — the overflowing produce of the winepress. יֶקֶב (yeqev, "winepress, wine vat"). The simile is generous: even though Levites own no land and grow no crops, their tithe-offering is valued as highly as if they were giving directly from their own threshing-floor and winepress. **LXX:** καὶ λογισθήσεται ὑμῖν τὰ ἀφαιρέματα ὑμῶν ὡς σῖτος ἀπὸ ἅλωνος καὶ ἀφαίρεμα ἀπὸ ληνοῦ ("and your contributions shall be reckoned to you as grain from the threshing-floor and as a contribution from the winepress").

**28** So [ken] you-shall-lift-up [tarimu] — also-you [gam attem] — a-contribution of [terumat] YHWH from-all [mi-kol] your-tithes [ma'seroteikhem] which [asher] you-shall-take [tiqkhu] from-the-Sons of [me-et benei] Israel [Yisra'el]; and-you-shall-give [u-netattem] from-it [mi-mennu] the-contribution of [et terumat] YHWH to-Aaron [le-Aharon] the-priest [ha-kohen].

> **Key terms:** כֵּן תָּרִימוּ גַם־אַתֶּם תְּרוּמַת יְהוָה (ken tarimu gam attem terumat YHWH) = "so you shall lift up — also you — a contribution of YHWH" — the emphatic גַם אַתֶּם (gam attem, "also you, you too") stresses that the Levites, despite being recipients of the people's tithes, must themselves be givers. No one in Israel is exempt from contributing to YHWH. מִכֹּל מַעְשְׂרֹתֵיכֶם (mi-kol ma'seroteikhem) = "from all your tithes" — the plural מַעְשְׂרוֹת (ma'serot, "tithes") indicating the totality of what they receive. וּנְתַתֶּם מִמֶּנּוּ אֶת־תְּרוּמַת יְהוָה לְאַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן (u-netattem mi-mennu et terumat YHWH le-Aharon ha-kohen) = "and you shall give from it the contribution of YHWH to Aaron the priest" — the ultimate recipient of the tithe-of-the-tithe is Aaron (= the Aaronic priesthood). The chain of giving runs: Israel -> Levites -> priests -> YHWH. **LXX:** καὶ δώσετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἀφαίρεμα κυρίῳ Ααρων τῷ ἱερεῖ ("and you shall give from it a contribution to the Lord — to Aaron the priest").

**29** From-all [mi-kol] your-gifts [mattnotekhem] you-shall-lift-up [tarimu] every [et kol] contribution of [terumat] YHWH; from-all [mi-kol] its-best [khelbo] — its-consecrated-portion [et miqdosho] from-it [mi-mennu].'

> **Key terms:** מִכֹּל מַתְּנֹתֵיכֶם תָּרִימוּ אֵת כָּל־תְּרוּמַת יְהוָה (mi-kol mattnotekhem tarimu et kol terumat YHWH) = "from all your gifts you shall lift up every contribution of YHWH" — מַתָּנוֹת (mattanot, "gifts") from נתן. The principle is restated: from everything the Levites receive, the best must be given to YHWH. מִכָּל־חֶלְבּוֹ אֶת־מִקְדְּשׁוֹ מִמֶּנּוּ (mi-kol khelbo et miqdosho mi-mennu) = "from all its best — its consecrated portion from it" — חֵלֶב (khelev, "fat, best") again means the finest part, and מִקְדָּשׁ (miqdash, "consecrated portion, sanctuary") here means "its holy portion" — that which is set apart from the whole. **LXX:** ἀπὸ παντὸς τοῦ στέατος αὐτοῦ τὸ ἡγιασμένον ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ("from all its fat — the sanctified portion from it").

**30** And-you-shall-say [ve-amarta] unto-them [aleihem], 'When-you-lift-up [be-harimkhem] its-best [et khelbo] from-it [mi-mennu], and-it-shall-be-reckoned [ve-nekhshav] to-the-Levites [la-Levi'im] as-the-produce of [ki-tevuat] a-threshing-floor [goren] and-as-the-produce of [u-khi-tevuat] a-winepress [yaqev].

> **Key terms:** וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם (ve-amarta aleihem) = "and you shall say to them" — Moses continues relaying YHWH's instructions. בַּהֲרִימְכֶם אֶת־חֶלְבּוֹ מִמֶּנּוּ (ba-harimkhem et khelbo mi-mennu) = "when you lift up its best from it" — the hifil infinitive construct of רום with 2mp suffix + preposition בְּ (be, "when, in"). Once the Levites give the best tenth to the priests, the remainder is credited to them. וְנֶחְשַׁב לַלְוִיִּם כִּתְבוּאַת גֹּרֶן וְכִתְבוּאַת יָקֶב (ve-nekhshav la-Levi'im ki-tevuat goren u-khi-tevuat yaqev) = "and it shall be reckoned to the Levites as the produce of a threshing-floor and as the produce of a winepress" — תְּבוּאָה (tevu'ah, "produce, yield, income") from בוא (bo, "to come") — "that which comes in." The remaining nine-tenths of the tithe becomes the Levites' ordinary, non-sacred income — they may use it freely, like a farmer uses the produce of his fields. **LXX:** καὶ λογισθήσεται τοῖς Λευίταις ὡς γένημα ἀπὸ ἅλωνος καὶ ὡς γένημα ἀπὸ ληνοῦ ("and it shall be reckoned to the Levites as produce from the threshing-floor and as produce from the winepress").

**31** And-you-shall-eat [va-akhaltem] it [oto] in-every [be-khol] place [maqom] — you [attem] and-your-households [u-veite-khem]; for [ki] a-wage [sakhar] it [hu] for-you [lakhem] in-exchange-for [khelef] your-service [avodatkhem] in-the-Tent of [be-ohel] Meeting [mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** וַאֲכַלְתֶּם אֹתוֹ בְּכָל־מָקוֹם (va-akhaltem oto be-khol maqom) = "and you shall eat it in every place" — unlike the most holy offerings that must be eaten in the sacred precincts (v. 10), the Levitical tithe (after the priestly tenth is removed) may be eaten anywhere — בְּכָל מָקוֹם (be-khol maqom, "in every place"). It is now ordinary, non-sacred food. אַתֶּם וּבֵיתֵכֶם (attem u-veite-khem) = "you and your households" — the entire Levitical family, including women, children, and servants, may eat from it. כִּי־שָׂכָר הוּא לָכֶם חֵלֶף עֲבֹדַתְכֶם בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ki sakhar hu lakhem khelef avodatkhem be-ohel mo'ed) = "for it is a wage for you in exchange for your service in the Tent of Meeting" — שָׂכָר (sakhar, "wage, hire, reward") from שׂכר (sakhar, "to hire"). חֵלֶף (khelef, "in exchange for") — the same word as v. 21. The tithe is not a gift or handout but a divinely mandated wage for sacred labor. **LXX:** καὶ φάγεσθε αὐτὰ ἐν παντὶ τόπῳ, ὑμεῖς καὶ οἱ οἶκοι ὑμῶν, ὅτι μισθὸς οὗτος ὑμῖν ἀντὶ τῶν λειτουργιῶν ὑμῶν τῶν ἐν τῇ σκηνῇ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and you shall eat them in every place, you and your houses, for this is your wage in return for your services in the tent of the testimony").

**32** And-not [ve-lo] shall-you-bear [tis'u] upon-it [alav] sin [khet] when-you-lift-up [be-harimkhem] its-best [et khelbo] from-it [mi-mennu]; and-the-holy-things of [ve-et qodshei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] you-shall-not [lo] profane [tekhalelu], and-not [ve-lo] shall-you-die [tamutu]."

> **Key terms:** וְלֹא־תִשְׂאוּ עָלָיו חֵטְא (ve-lo tis'u alav khet) = "and you shall not bear sin upon it" — the condition: as long as the Levites faithfully give the best tenth to the priests, they incur no guilt for consuming the remainder. The verb נשׂא (nasa, "to bear") + חֵטְא (khet, "sin") — the same construction as v. 22. בַּהֲרִימְכֶם אֶת־חֶלְבּוֹ מִמֶּנּוּ (ba-harimkhem et khelbo mi-mennu) = "when you lift up its best from it" — the condition is the faithful giving of the best portion. If they fail to give the priestly tenth, or if they give inferior produce rather than the best, they bear sin. וְאֶת־קָדְשֵׁי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא תְחַלְּלוּ (ve-et qodshei benei Yisra'el lo tekhalelu) = "and the holy things of the Sons of Israel you shall not profane" — the piel of חלל (khalal, "to profane, pollute, defile") — the opposite of קדשׁ (qadash, "to make holy"). To mishandle the holy things — whether by consuming the priestly portion, by offering inferior produce, or by treating sacred things as common — is to profane them. וְלֹא תָמוּתוּ (ve-lo tamutu) = "and you shall not die" — the qal imperfect 2mp of מות (mut, "to die"). The verse ends with the ultimate warning: obey, and live; profane the holy, and die. This conclusion bookends the chapter's opening (v. 1), which began with "bearing the iniquity of the sanctuary." The entire priestly-Levitical system is bracketed by the gravity of sacred responsibility. **LXX:** καὶ τὰ ἅγια τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ οὐ βεβηλώσετε, ἵνα μὴ ἀποθάνητε ("and you shall not profane the holy things of the sons of Israel, lest you die") — ἵνα μή ("lest") making explicit the consequence clause.

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Pages 170-171

1. **Direct divine address to Aaron (vv. 1-24):** The Leningrad Codex preserves the remarkable feature of YHWH speaking directly to Aaron throughout the first twenty-four verses. The shift to Moses in v. 25 (va-yedabber YHWH el Mosheh) is clearly marked in the manuscript. This extended direct address to Aaron is nearly unique in the Pentateuch and underscores the priestly nature of the legislation.

2. **Setumah and petuhah divisions:** The parashah spacing in the Leningrad Codex marks clear section breaks corresponding to the four major thematic units: vv. 1-7 (priestly-Levitical duties), vv. 8-20 (priestly portions), vv. 21-24 (Levitical tithe), and vv. 25-32 (tithe of the tithe). These ancient paragraph markers confirm the literary structure recognized by modern scholars.

3. **The phrase avodat mattanah (vv. 6-7):** The expression עֲבֹדַת מַתָּנָה ("service of gift") is unique to this chapter and captures the dual nature of sacred vocation: it is simultaneously labor and grace, duty and privilege, burden and blessing. The Leningrad Codex's vocalization confirms the construct chain reading.

4. **Berit melakh olam (v. 19):** The "covenant of salt, perpetual" (בְּרִית מֶלַח עוֹלָם) is one of only two explicit "salt covenants" in the Hebrew Bible (cf. 2 Chron 13:5). The Leningrad Codex's vocalization is clear, with no textual variants in the manuscript tradition. Salt's preservative quality makes it the ideal metaphor for an incorruptible, enduring covenant.

5. **Ani khelqekha ve-nakhalatekha (v. 20):** The statement "I am your portion and your inheritance" (אֲנִי חֶלְקְךָ וְנַחֲלָתְךָ) is one of the Torah's most theologically dense utterances. The Leningrad Codex preserves the first-person divine pronoun אֲנִי (ani, "I") with full clarity. This verse becomes the fountainhead of the entire biblical tradition of YHWH as the believer's "portion" (cf. Ps 16:5, 73:26, 119:57, Lam 3:24).

6. **Padoh tifdeh (v. 15):** The infinitive absolute construction פָּדֹה תִפְדֶּה ("redeeming, you shall redeem") is clearly vocalized in the Leningrad Codex, with the infinitive absolute פָּדֹה preceding the finite verb תִפְדֶּה, producing the emphatic doubling required by the EMET binding conventions.

7. **Ma'aser min ha-ma'aser (v. 26):** The expression "a tithe from the tithe" (מַעֲשֵׂר מִן הַמַּעֲשֵׂר) establishes the mathematical principle of the tithe-of-the-tithe — the Levites give one tenth of their received tenth, meaning the priests ultimately receive one hundredth of the total Israelite produce. The Leningrad Codex's spacing makes the phrase unmistakable.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

8. **Terumah / aparche translation:** Throughout the chapter, the Vaticanus consistently renders Hebrew תְּרוּמָה (terumah, "contribution/heave-offering") with Greek ἀφαίρεμα (aphairema, "that which is taken away/set apart") or ἀπαρχή (aparkhe, "firstfruits"), reflecting the LXX translators' understanding that the terumah is a portion "removed" from the whole rather than "lifted up" (the literal Hebrew sense).

9. **Skene tou martyriou vs. ohel mo'ed:** As noted throughout this translation project, the Vaticanus consistently renders אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed, "Tent of Meeting") as σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου (skene tou martyriou, "Tent of the Testimony/Witness"), following the LXX's interpretive tradition that emphasizes the Ark of the Testimony housed within rather than the meeting function of the space.

10. **Verse 5 — hamartia for qetsef:** In v. 5, the Vaticanus reads ἁμαρτία ("sin") where the MT has קֶצֶף ("wrath"). This is a significant theological divergence: the Hebrew says the priestly guard prevents divine wrath from breaking out; the Greek says it prevents sin from occurring. The LXX reflects a different causal understanding — the priests guard against human transgression rather than divine reaction.

11. **Verse 22 — thanatephoros:** The Vaticanus's θανατηφόρον ("death-bearing") in v. 22 is a striking compound adjective that compresses the Hebrew's two infinitives (לָשֵׂאת חֵטְא לָמוּת, "to bear sin, to die") into a single word, creating a more compact but less nuanced expression.

12. **Verse 7 — leitourgia domatos:** The Vaticanus renders עֲבֹדַת מַתָּנָה (avodat mattanah) as λειτουργίαν δόματος (leitourgian domatos, "a liturgical service of gift"), employing the Greek term λειτουργία from which English "liturgy" derives. The word originally denoted public service performed at one's own expense — a resonant parallel to the priestly vocation as both service and gift.

### Theological Synthesis

13. **The chapter as divine charter:** Numbers 18 functions as the constitutional document of the Israelite priestly-Levitical system. Coming immediately after the Korah rebellion (ch. 16), the plague (ch. 17:1-15), and the budding rod (ch. 17:16-28), it answers the question that has been building through the wilderness narrative: Who may approach YHWH, and on what terms? The answer is layered: the priests serve at the altar and within the veil; the Levites serve in the outer precincts as a protective buffer; and the people support both through tithes and offerings while remaining at a safe distance. The system is not about exclusion but about protection — the Levites and priests absorb the danger of holiness so that the people may live.

14. **The economics of sacred service:** The chapter establishes a complete economic system for a landless priestly-Levitical class: priests receive the most holy portions (eaten in the sacred precincts by males only), lesser holy portions (eaten by their entire households), firstfruits, firstborn redemption payments, and the tithe-of-the-tithe; Levites receive the general tithe. This is framed not as taxation but as divinely mandated wages (שָׂכָר, sakhar, v. 31) for sacred labor — and as YHWH's own gift (מַתָּנָה, mattanah) to His servants.
